sentences.text: Yeah.
Right.
This was March 18, 1943, and I knew that my mother is going to stay during the night with my brother in the bunker.
I came back from work.
It was a very unpleasant March.
It was raining and snowing.
It was dark already.
I came back from work.
It was completely dark, and I came-- I was going towards my-- where, where we lived.
And, and I had to cross a yard to get into this room that we shared with some other people.
A small ghetto with just a few streets.
Very miserable.
The poorest in the city.
I remember the mud.
I remember the darkness.
And I-- as I was approaching the door, I heard this terrible scream of my mother.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] This-- I'll-- I-- I'll never get rid of this picture and feeling of-- sinking feeling.
Not only inside me, but I've heard that everything is finished.
I knew-- I knew that it must have something to do with my brother.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So what I learned was that as my mother was coming back from work ready to, to join the boys, they were let out by Germans.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They were found and she saw-- she saw him [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] being led to his death.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
Now, I was young in the ghetto, and I think I was lucky.
I can't imagine what it was like to be a mother in the ghetto.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Anyhow, so this is what happened.
They were-- they were killed in the Jewish cemetery the next day in the morning.
I don't know what was happening to them during that night.
They spent a night in jail, apparently, where they tortured by the Gestapo.
I don't know.
Of course they had arms with them.
They had pistols and, and grenades.
Were they-- did they have to dig their own grave?
I don't know, and I'll never know.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Somehow I never stopped mourning my brother.
I don't know why I feel differently with the death of my father and the death of my mother.
But this young life-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he was 19.
He was going to be 20 in one month.
Well, my mother was insane the first few hours.
I had problems calming her down.
I kept telling her, no-- [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] --it was not him.
It was not him.
Maybe it was not him.
She gradually calmed down.
She had to live for me.
And we never talked about it anymore.
And this is another thing that I regret, because she must have known more about how they were actually discovered.
There are two versions.
One is that at least-- a little Jewish boy who was hidden actually in the bunker, somewhere around there, went out.
And he was spotted by S men-- SS men coming by.
And they started to--
--beat him up, and tell us where-- who's here, and where are you, and where is your mother, and is there anybody else around here.
And he gave them up.
This is one version.
Another version that I read in some books-- but it's all hearsay.
Nobody knows exactly.
Another version was that perhaps they did draw in apparently-- they did draw in some group.
But this group of partisans didn't quite trust them.
And they told them, well, all right, you're going.
And some action was being prepared for them in the city.
You'll go there, but you have to give us the address where you'll be.
And there is this version that this is how they were given up.
But I don't know what the truth is.
It just shows you the possibilities.
Everything was possible in Poland during the war.
I don't remember--
--exactly.
But very soon I started to work in the HASAG that later on became, became the labor camp.
And so did my mother.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Well, we-- so before that happened, before this March, of course, there were other things happening in the-- in the small ghetto.
As I said, the small ghetto was just a killing ground; just terrible, constant [NON-ENGLISH], selections.
And on January the 4th, I remember this one very well.
And this was-- my brother was already away but still in life.
They didn't take anybody to work.
They told us to stay in the ghetto, so we knew that things are going to happen.
And they told us to-- everybody to, to come to this so-called [INAUDIBLE], which is the old marketplace, a small place.
My mother said, we are not going.
She tried-- from there on, we tried to hide as much as possible.
So everybody went.
We went up through the attic.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a fairly plain attic.
Some other people were there, too, just counting on their luck that nobody maybe will come.
And I believe-- I believe so, that there was a tiny window there from which I could see what's going on in the marketplace.
But when I think about it, it may just as well be what I heard from other people again and again.
And I have the picture-- and I don't know.
I don't remember if I really saw it, or was it from all these tales about what was happening.
So what I think I saw was this crowd of people in complete silence, standing there in the small market.
This was a little, miserable square.
And the house where we were hiding was bordering this square.
And all we heard there, and this we heard, was screaming of the Germans, some orders being given.
And what I saw, or I think I saw, was a group of Germans, maybe 10 of them, including Degenhardt.
This was the ruler of the Tschenstochau ghetto.
He's the man who liquidated-- who was was in charge of liquidating the Jews.
Yes.
Oh, yes, he was a German SS man.
Yeah.
And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and the Judenrat people were standing there.
And they were making a selection-- pulling out people that they didn't need anymore.
The crowd was getting bigger and bigger.
Yeah-- was pulled out.
He was very well known in the ghetto.
He was a wonderful man.
He was cooking kettles of soup every Saturday to feed the poor, so they knew him.
And somebody from the Judenrat when he was pulled out as an older man, he claimed him.
And then he was pulled out again, and again he was reclaimed.
And then what happened was that two young men threw themselves on the Germans with pistols.
One of them was Isjo Finer, who was a friend of my brother from school, and the other one was Mendel Fisch-Levizcs.
And they had pistols.
And apparently these pistols jammed, so they were killed on the spot.
Yes.
And after that, they took every tenth from this crowd to be finished off, killed.
And Mr.
Vryman was also detained.
So twice he escaped, and this time he was taken.
These people were killed almost immediately.
Some of them right on the spot there.
And others in the-- at the cemetery.
There were a few hundred people that died on that day.
The Vryman family was still alive.
His wife, his two children, his son, who was married and had a baby, they hid themselves.
They were in hiding during the big action.
So the, the wife-- the following day, his wife took the baby and went to the train with the baby.
The two children survived.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS]
Yeah.
So I'm coming to that, yeah.
So I'm coming to that.
So I told you about the March, the killing of my brother.
And pretty soon, in June 1943, the ghetto was liquidated [SIGHS] and a lot of people again selected out, too.
But we were-- did not go through the selection-- You were in hiding?
--on that day.
No, we were working in the HASAG.
So we were kept there.
At that stage, we knew a lot, yeah.
They were going to Treblinka.
And we also knew that they were being shot at the Jewish cemetery.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Most of them were going to Treblinka.
Well, so we found ourself in the HASAG.
So there was only 2,000 or 3,000 of us left, the workers that were necessary to run the, the German war industry.
But that same night, there was a selection [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] in the HASAG.
Too-- apparently too many people made their way there, and this we could not escape.
I don't know if it was that same night, or a few nights later.
It was at night.
It was at 10 o'clock in the evening.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they told us to march, six of us in a row.
The place was illuminated.
It was 10 o'clock in the evening, but it was bright, bright light.
And this time, in this selection, there were the the werkschutz the people that were guarding us in the camp, who took part in it.
And the foreman, the German foreman, the civilian workers, who worked for this place.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And as we were marching through, of course, my mother, after the death of her son, was ruined.
And I was-- there were three people.
She was the fourth.
I was the fifth, I believe.
And, I, I don't know.
It was a moment, just one moment.
My mother was pulled out by a-- by a civilian foreman.
She was pulled out, and I ran after her.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I, I don't know.
I didn't think at that point.
I-- It was just-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I just ran after my mother.
[SIGHS] We were collected at the site, and the group was getting bigger and bigger-- older people, some children.
And my mother despairing that I was there, and why did I come.
We were put in some little-- I believe it was a little factory hall-- until the entire group was collected, the selection was finished.
And then we were led out.
And as we stood there, I thought, it was close to a wall.
And I was sure that they are going to shoot us there.
Somehow I-- all the werkschutz people were there with their-- with their machine guns.
And I thought, that's it.
It's strange, because I remember having some amazing clarity of mind.
I don't remember any great fear.
I, I saw my short life, somehow, and I thought about, about my name that I left on my school desk, you know, like children do--
--this type of thing.
Yes.
And I, I think I thought, well, this is what's going to be left.
And then my mother pulled me, and she started talking to the Werkschutzleiter, who was a sadist.
His name, I believe, was Clem.
And she was telling him, let her go.
She was not pulled out.
She's-- you know.
And when I heard it, I said, no.
I didn't know any German.
So I just said, [GERMAN], only the two of us.
And he let us go.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he told us to go, and I didn't believe him for a moment.
My mother started to pull me and to run.
And I told her, no, I don't want.
They'll-- he'll shoot us from the back.
I don't want it.
But then nobody was shooting, and we went.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we came to, to this-- the barracks were not built yet, because it was the very beginning of the camp.
And we were sleeping on some bunks in a factory hall.
And I remember coming there and lots of people crying, sitting there and crying.
And we approached our bunk and there were some people who knew us.
And there was a young woman who was sitting on the top bunk and crying-- because we were not there.
And when she saw us, she fell off the bunk.
And we started to laugh.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
So the next day was like, like a dream.
I was not quite, I don't remember.
I was working.
I was working in the camp on a machine that was calibrating the cartridges.
It was not hard work.
But we were, of course, guarded constantly, and harassed, and beaten up occasionally.
I only remember this day following this incident during our breaks.
The [INAUDIBLE] people would come to look at me.
That my mother, such a wonder, your child went after her mother.
When many mothers--
And survived.
And survived.
And how-- what happened to this man?
Why did he-- he was a sadist.
He-- killing for him was nothing.
I don't know.
I'll never know.
Well, anyhow, so we survived.
And we worked there.
There were some Poles working there.

It was interesting, because there were some-- I remember close to my machine there was a long table where four or five young, good looking Polish girls-- maybe they were [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] German volksdeutsche-- you know what it is-- but they were Poles.
And in the beginning, they looked at us, and I could see that they looked at us with some horror and some pity.
And as the time went on, they started to look through us.
They didn't see us anymore.
They got used.
It was a young Polish foreman who was a specialist of this particular machine that I worked on.
And in the beginning, he was friendly.
He was good.
Thadick. ?] And then he got completely depraved.
He was depraved by another Polish, she was a volksdeutsche, [?
Erika ?] [?
Uraina, ?] who was sleeping around with Germans, and with him, too.
And then he was just like everybody else.
Well, yes, there was a little bit of that.
Yes, there was a little bit of that.
So we knew that the Russians are approaching.
We knew about how to survive now.
In the meantime, we spent two years, still two years to go in the camp.
We worked.
We starved.
We-- many of us, of course, were pulled out, and killed, and murdered, and sent out in transport.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] How to survive was the question.
Well, at the end, we started to hear the Po-- the, the Russian artillery, the Russian Katyusha.
It lasted a few days.
This was already January, January, 1945.
And we started to hear the Katyushas.
And two weeks before it all ended, we were-- my mother and I lost our jobs, which meant we were in a group of people that was being collected to, to be-- to be sent.
Now, these lists were made by Jewish kapos.
No.
And well, even if I know, I'm not talking about it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She was a young woman.
She was from the same school that we were.
She was one year older than my-- than my brother.
She was she was not bad, actually.
She was asked to make a list, and she had to make a list.
So she picked up a person who was older and a person who was younger, and sort of did not fit and may die anyway.
I don't-- Anyhow, at that point we were waiting for a transport.
We were just living for a few more days maybe.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we lost our food rations, so we were starved.
And we did something, I believe cleaning potatoes.
They were frozen potatoes.
They put us there, so we ate the raw [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] skinned potato skins.
But then the 16th came, the 16th of January.
And on the 16th of January, it was clear that the Russians are very, very close.
We heard them, and, and the Germans started to panic.
The wagons were there.
It was behind camp, there was a train being prepared for us.
The Germans took a lot of men with them.
They ended up in Buchenwald and all types of places, and did the death march, and not many of them came back.
But we hid again with my mother.
And the last panic and the last collection of people to be thrown into the--
Oh, yeah.
We, we-- I don't even remember where.
We were hiding just in little corners, just behind machines, behind big soup kettles, I remember, in the kitchen.
We were hiding.
And as the 16th, as the evening came, it was quiet.
It became to be very quiet.
Then around midnight, it was completely quiet, and there was nobody in this place except the two of us.
And my mother said, come, we'll see what's happening.
We started to go, and there was nobody.
And there were no Germans.
And we started to walk.
And we came out of the factory grounds where the Germans lived, and there was nobody.
And we just walked like lunatics.
We just walked in the night.
This was already early morning, 17th.
And we walked through the camp gate, and we were out.
And we started to walk.
We were in rags.
But I didn't tell you, of course, how we were freezing there.
How we were kept after 12 hours of work, standing in the snow for-- my legs were both frostbitten.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were in wooden clogs.
We had numbers here, and all types of things.
But we started to walk.
This was a very, very cold January 17th.
Yeah.
And there were thousands of people walking from all over again-- people returning, people going.
Nobody paid any attention to us.
But what is the strangest is that the Wehrmacht, the German army, was walking out of the city.
They were in complete silence, walking out of the city on the road.
I only heard the sound of the snow under their boots.
And all of us were on the sidewalks, walking.
Not a word, not a shot, nothing.
They marched out of the city that very day.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So this was the 17th of January, and we were liberated by the Russians.
[SIGHS]
Well, we went-- first steps was into a home.
My mother knew some people there, so we went in.
And they, of course, took us in.
They gave us some food.
I didn't know.
I was like in a dream.
And then in the morning, in the early morning, I saw the-- these first Soviet tanks.
